sections.label: Section 1
sections.original: Ὦ Βρόμιε , διὰ σὲ μυρίους ἔχω πόνους
sections.translation: O Bromius , unnumbered are the toils I bear because of thee , no less now than when I was young and hale ; first , when thou wert driven mad by Hera and didst leave the mountain nymphs , thy nurses ;

sections.label: Section 5
sections.original: ἔπειτά γ ’ ἀμφὶ γηγενῆ μάχην δορὸς
sections.translation: next , when in battle with earth-born spearmen I stood beside thee on the right as squire , and slew Enceladus , smiting him full in the middle of his targe with my spear . Come , though , let me see ; must I confess ’ twas all a dream ? No , by Zeus ! since I really showed his spoils to the Bacchic god .

sections.label: Section 10
sections.original: καὶ νῦν ἐκείνων μείζον ’ ἐξαντλῶ πόνον .
sections.translation: And now am I enduring to the full a toil still worse than those . For when Hera sent forth a race of Tyrrhene pirates against thee , that thou mightest be smuggled far away , I , as soon as the news reached me , sailed in quest of thee with my children ; and , taking the helm

sections.label: Section 15
sections.original: αὐτὸς λαβὼν ηὔθυνον ἀμφῆρες δόρυ ,
sections.translation: myself , I stood on the end of the stern and steered our trim craft ; and my sons , sitting at the oars , made the grey billows froth and foam as they sought thee , my liege . But just as we had come nigh Malea in our course , an east-wind blew upon the ship

sections.label: Section 20
sections.original: ἐξέβαλεν ἡμᾶς τήνδ ’ ἐς Αἰτναίαν πέτραν ,
sections.translation: and drove us hither to the rock of Aetna , where in lonely caverns dwell the one-eyed children of ocean ’ s god , the murdering Cyclopes . Captured by one of them we are slaves in his house ; Polyphemus they call him whom we serve ;

sections.label: Section 25
sections.original: Πολύφημον · ἀντὶ δ ’ εὐίων βακχευμάτων
sections.translation: and instead of Bacchic revelry we are herding a godless Cyclops ’ s flocks ; and so it is my children , striplings as they are , tend the young thereof on the edge of the downs ; while my appointed task is to stay here and fill the troughs and sweep out the cave ,

sections.label: Section 30
sections.original: μένων τέταγμαι τάσδε , τῷδε δυσσεβεῖ
sections.translation: or wait upon the ungodly Cyclops at his impious feasts . His orders now compel obedience ; I have to scrape out his house with the rake you see , so as to receive the Cyclops , my absent master ,

sections.label: Section 35
sections.original: καθαροῖσιν ἄντροις μῆλά τ ’ ἐσδεχώμεθα .
sections.translation: and his sheep in clean caverns .

sections.label: Section 36
sections.original: ἤδη δὲ παῖδας προσνέμοντας εἰσορῶ
sections.translation: But already I see my children driving their browsing flocks towards me .

sections.label: Section 37
sections.original: ποίμνας . τί ταῦτα ; μῶν κρότος σικινίδων
sections.translation: What means this ? is the beat of feet in the Sicinnis dance the same to you now as when ye attended the Bacchic god in his revelries

sections.label: Section 40
sections.original: προσῇτ ’ ἀοιδαῖς βαρβίτων σαυλούμενοι ;
sections.translation: and made your way with dainty steps to the mask of lyres to the halls of Althaea ?

sections.label: Section 41
sections.original: παῖ γενναίων μὲν πατέρων
sections.translation: Offspring of well-bred sires and dams , pray whither wilt thou be gone from me to the rocks ? Hast thou not here a gentle breeze ,

sections.label: Section 45
sections.original: καὶ ποιηρὰ βοτάνα ;
sections.translation: and grass to browse , and water from the eddying stream set near the cave in troughs ? and are not thy young ones bleating for thee ?

sections.label: Section 49
sections.original: — ψύττ ’· οὐ τᾷδ ’ οὔ ; οὐ τᾷδε νεμῇ
sections.translation: Pst ! pst ! wilt thou not browse here ,

sections.label: Section 50
sections.original: κλιτὺν δροσεράν ;
sections.translation: here on the dewy slope ? Ho ? ho ! ere long will I cast a stone at thee . Away , away ! O horned one , to the fold-keeper of the Cyclops , the country-ranging shepherd .

sections.label: Section 55
sections.original: σπαργῶντας μαστοὺς χάλασον ·
sections.translation: Loosen thy bursting udder ; welcome to thy teats the kids , whom thou leavest in the lambkins ’ pens . Those little bleating kids , asleep the livelong day , miss thee ;

sections.label: Section 60
sections.original: εἰς αὐλὰν πότ ’ ἀμφιβαλεῖς
sections.translation: wilt then leave at last the rich grass pastures on the peaks of Aetna and enter the fold ?

sections.label: Section 62a
sections.original: — νύττ ’· οὐ τᾷδ ’ οὔ ; οὐ τᾷδε νεμῇ

sections.label: Section 63
sections.original: οὐ τάδε Βρόμιος , οὐ τάδε χοροὶ
sections.translation: Here we have no Bromian god ; no dances here , or Bacchantes thyrsus-bearing ;

sections.label: Section 65
sections.original: οὐ τυμπάνων ἀλαλαγμοὶ
sections.translation: no roll of drums , or drops of sparkling wine by gurgling founts ; nor is it now with Nymphs in Nysa I sing a song of Bacchus , Bacchus !

sections.label: Section 70
sections.original: μέλπω πρὸς τὰν Ἀφροδίταν ,
sections.translation: to the queen of love , in quest of whom I once sped on with Bacchantes , white of foot .

sections.label: Section 73
sections.original: ὦ φίλος ·
sections.translation: Dear friend , dear Bacchic god , whither art roaming alone ,

sections.label: Section 75
sections.original: ποῖ ξανθὰν χαίταν σείεις ;
sections.translation: waving thy auburn locks , while I , thy minister , do service to the one-eyed Cyclops , a slave and wanderer I ,

sections.label: Section 80
sections.original: σὺν τᾷδε τράγου χλαίνᾳ μελέᾳ
sections.translation: clad in this wretched goat-skin dress , severed from thy love ?

sections.label: Section 82
sections.original: σιγήσατ ’, ὦ τέκν ’, ἄντρα δ ’ ἐς πετρηρεφῆ
sections.translation: Hush , children ! and bid our servants fold the flocks in the rock-roofed cavern .

sections.label: Section 84
sections.original: χωρεῖτ ’· ἀτὰρ δὴ τίνα , πάτερ , σπουδὴν ἔχεις ;
sections.translation: ( To Servants .) Away ! ( To Silenus .) But prithee , why such haste , father ?

sections.label: Section 85
sections.original: ὁρῶ πρὸς ἀκταῖς ναὸς Ἑλλάδος σκάφος
sections.translation: I see the hull of a ship from Hellas at the shore , and men , that wield the oar , on their way to this cave with some chieftain . About their necks they carry empty vessels and pitchers for water ; they are in want of food . Luckless strangers !

sections.label: Section 90
sections.original: τίνες ποτ ’ εἰσίν ; οὐκ ἴσασι δεσπότην
sections.translation: who can they be ? They know not what manner of man our master Polyphemus is , to have set foot here in his cheerless abode and come to the jaws of the cannibal Cyclops in an evil hour . But hold ye your peace , that we may inquire

sections.label: Section 95
sections.original: πόθεν πάρεισι Σικελὸν Αἰτναῖον πάγον .
sections.translation: whence they come to the peak of Sicilian Aetna .

sections.label: Section 96
sections.original: ξένοι , φράσαιτ ’ ἂν νᾶμα ποτάμιον πόθεν
sections.translation: Pray tell us , sirs , of some river-spring whence we might draw a draught to slake our thirst , or of someone willing to sell victuals to mariners in need .

sections.label: Section 99
sections.original: -- τί χρῆμα ; Βρομίου πόλιν ἔοιγμεν ἐσβαλεῖν ·
sections.translation: Why , what is this ? We seem to have chanced upon a city of the Bromian god ;

sections.label: Section 100
sections.original: Σατύρων πρὸς ἄντροις τόνδ ’ ὅμιλον εἰσορῶ .
sections.translation: here by the caves I see a group of Satyrs . To the eldest first I bid All hail !

sections.label: Section 102
sections.original: χαῖρ ’, ὦ ξέν ’, ὅστις δ ’ εἶ φράσον πάτραν τε σήν .
sections.translation: All hail , sir ! tell me who thou art , and name thy country .

sections.label: Section 103
sections.original: Ἴθακος Ὀδυσσεύς , γῆς Κεφαλλήνων ἄναξ .
sections.translation: Odysseus of Ithaca , king of the Cephallenians ’ land .

sections.label: Section 104
sections.original: οἶδ ’ ἄνδρα , κρόταλον δριμύ , Σισύφου γένος .
sections.translation: I know him for a prating knave , one of Sisyphus ’ shrewd offspring .

sections.label: Section 105
sections.original: ἐκεῖνος οὗτός εἰμι · λοιδόρει δὲ μή ·
sections.translation: I am the man ; abuse me not .

sections.label: Section 106
sections.original: πόθεν Σικελίαν τήνδε ναυστολῶν πάρει ;
sections.translation: Whence hast thou sailed hither to Sicily ?

sections.label: Section 107
sections.original: ἐξ Ἰλίου γε κἀπὸ Τρωικῶν πόνων .
sections.translation: From Ilium and the toils of Troy .

sections.label: Section 108
sections.original: πῶς ; πορθμὸν οὐκ ᾔδησθα πατρῴας χθονός ;
sections.translation: How was that ? didst thou not know the passage to thy native land ?

sections.label: Section 109
sections.original: ἀνέμων θύελλαι δεῦρό μ ’ ἥρπασαν βίᾳ .
sections.translation: Tempestuous winds drove me hither against my will .

sections.label: Section 110
sections.original: παπαῖ · τὸν αὐτὸν δαίμον ’ ἐξαντλεῖς ἐμοί .
sections.translation: God wot ! thou art in the same plight as I am .

sections.label: Section 111
sections.original: ἦ καὶ σὺ δεῦρο πρὸς βίαν ἀπεστάλης ;
sections.translation: Why , wert thou too drifted hither against thy will ?

sections.label: Section 112
sections.original: λῃστὰς διώκων οἳ Βρόμιον ἀνήρπασαν .
sections.translation: I was , as I pursued the pirates who carried Bromius off .

sections.label: Section 113
sections.original: τίς δ ’ ἥδε χώρα καὶ τίνες ναίουσί νιν ;
sections.translation: What land is this and who are its inhabitants ?

sections.label: Section 114
sections.original: Αἰτναῖος ὄχθος Σικελίας ὑπέρτατος .
sections.translation: This is mount Aetna , the highest point in Sicily .

sections.label: Section 115
sections.original: τείχη δὲ ποῦ ’ στι καὶ πόλεως πυργώματα ;
sections.translation: But where are the city-walls and ramparts ?

sections.label: Section 116
sections.original: οὐκ εἴσ ’· ἔρημοι πρῶνες ἀνθρώπων , ξένε .
sections.translation: There are none ; the headlands , sir , are void of men .

sections.label: Section 117
sections.original: τίνες δ ’ ἔχουσι γαῖαν ; ἦ θηρῶν γένος ;
sections.translation: Who then possess the land ? the race of wild creatures ?

sections.label: Section 118
sections.original: Κύκλωπες , ἄντρ ’ ἔχοντες , οὐ στέγας δόμων .
sections.translation: The Cyclopes , who have caves , not roofed houses .

sections.label: Section 119
sections.original: τίνος κλύοντες ; ἢ δεδήμευται κράτος ;
sections.translation: Obedient unto whom ? or is the power in the people ’ s hands ?

sections.label: Section 120
sections.original: νομάδες · ἀκούει δ ’ οὐδὲν οὐδεὶς οὐδενός .
sections.translation: They are rovers ; no man obeys another in anything .

sections.label: Section 121
sections.original: σπείρουσι δ ’— ἢ τῷ ζῶσι ;— Δήμητρος στάχυν ;
sections.translation: Do they sow Demeter ’ s grain , or on what do they live ?

sections.label: Section 122
sections.original: γάλακτι καὶ τυροῖσι καὶ μήλων βορᾷ .
sections.translation: On milk and cheese and flesh of sheep .

sections.label: Section 123
sections.original: Βρομίου δὲ πῶμ ’ ἔχουσιν , ἀμπέλου ῥοαῖς ;
sections.translation: Have they the drink of Bromius , the juice of the vine ?

sections.label: Section 124
sections.original: ἥκιστα · τοιγὰρ ἄχορον οἰκοῦσι χθόνα .
sections.translation: No indeed ! and thus it is a joyless land they dwell in .

sections.label: Section 125
sections.original: φιλόξενοι δὲ χὥσιοι περὶ ξένους ;
sections.translation: Are they hospitable and reverent towards strangers ?

sections.label: Section 126
sections.original: γλυκύτατά φασι τὰ κρέα τοὺς ξένους φορεῖν .
sections.translation: Strangers , they say , supply the daintiest meat .

sections.label: Section 127
sections.original: τί φῄς ; βορᾷ χαίρουσιν ἀνθρωποκτόνῳ ;
sections.translation: What , do they delight in killing men and eating them ?

sections.label: Section 128
sections.original: οὐδεὶς μολὼν δεῦρ ’ ὅστις οὐ κατεσφάγη .
sections.translation: No one has ever arrived here without being butchered .

sections.label: Section 129
sections.original: αὐτὸς δὲ Κύκλωψ ποῦ ’ στιν ; ἦ δόμων ἔσω ;
sections.translation: Where is the Cyclops himself ? inside his dwelling ?
